summary: I was born in Council Bluffs, Iowa, in August 1928. My family lived there until I was about four years old before we moved to Phoenix, Arizona. Council Bluffs was my very first home, though I don't remember much from those early years.

My family moved to Phoenix, Arizona when I was four years old. I grew up there and started my formal music training, including piano and violin lessons. I played in a school band and got to hear dance bands when they came through town. It was in Phoenix that I first played the cornet and trumpet and became interested in swing music.

When I was about sixteen, my brother and I came to Los Angeles on a summer vacation and decided to stay because of the vibrant musical scene here. We still had a year left in high school, but we knew this was where we wanted to be to pursue professional music careers. Los Angeles, especially Central Avenue, was where the music was really happening.

During the time of the Works Progress Administration, there were many people employed in various teaching roles. In Phoenix, I remember attending an art school where I learned sculpting and painting, and I also had violin lessons from a local teacher employed through the WPA. The WPA provided opportunities that helped me develop my artistic and musical skills.

Pearl Harbor was a turning point during my youth. After the attack in December 1941, many men were drafted, which opened up opportunities for me to switch from bass tuba to cornet and then trumpet in the school band. The event indirectly influenced my musical path by changing the availability of instruments.

Harry James was the first trumpet player I really became aware of and admired. Growing up in Phoenix, his music was frequently played on the radio, and I thought he was a great player. He was a major influence on my early interest in swing and big band music.

Benny Goodman was one of the big band leaders I listened to a lot on the radio when I was young. Along with Harry James, his music shaped my early tastes in swing. I heard his band frequently while growing up in Phoenix, and it helped inspire my own musical ambitions.

Duke Ellington's music was also part of what I heard growing up, though not as much as Harry James or Benny Goodman. His big band sound was influential in the swing era, and I appreciated his work as part of the broader musical landscape I was absorbing. He was one of the key figures in the music I admired.

Stan Kenton was another bandleader I heard on the radio during my youth. His music was part of the mix of big band sounds that I enjoyed and that influenced my developing musical style. Though I didn't have as much exposure to him as to some others, he was still important in the scene.

Jimmie Lunceford was one of the bandleaders whose music I heard growing up in Phoenix. His band was part of the swing music scene that captivated me as a young musician. Though not as prominent on the radio as some others, his style contributed to my early musical influences.

Interestingly, I hadn't heard Louis Armstrong at all when I was growing up in Phoenix. His records weren't played on the radio there for some reason, so he didn't influence me early on like Harry James did. It wasn't until later that I became aware of Armstrong's music and significance.

Artie Shaw's band came through Phoenix on a one-night engagement when I was playing in a local club. They arrived early, and Roy Eldridge, who was with Shaw's band, came by and sat in with us, even playing drums and then trumpet. It was a memorable experience to meet them and have them listen to our band.

Roy Eldridge was with Artie Shaw's band when they came to Phoenix, and I had the chance to meet him. He was a wonderful person who even sat in with our band, playing drums and trumpet. His visit was a highlight for me as a young musician playing cornet in a local band.
